paper_title: TRACER: Trace-Based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM Classification

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Every call to an LLM classification endpoint produces a labeled input-output pair already retained in production logs. These pairs constitute a free, growing training set: a lightweight surrogate trained on them can absorb a significant portion of future traffic at near-zero marginal inference cost. The open questions are when the surrogate is reliable enough to deploy, what it handles versus defers, and how that boundary evolves as data accumulates. We introduce TRACER (Trace-based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing), an open-source system that trains ML surrogates on an LLM's own production traces and governs deployment through a parity gate: the surrogate is activated only when its agreement with the LLM exceeds a user-specified threshold α. To make the routing boundary transparent, TRACER generates interpretability artifacts describing which input regions the surrogate handles, where it plateaus, and why it defers. On a 77-class intent benchmark with a Sonnet 4.6 teacher, TRACER achieves 83-100% surrogate coverage depending on the quality target α; on a 150-class benchmark, the surrogate fully replaces the teacher. On a natural language inference task, the parity gate correctly refuses deployment because the embedding representation cannot support reliable separation. The system is available as open-source software.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: TRACER: Trace-Based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM Classification Adam Rida

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: 1. Introduction 6 2 0 2 6 1 ] . [ 1 1 3 5 4 1 . 4 0 6 2 : r Every call to an LLM classification endpoint produces labeled inputoutput pair already retained in production logs. These pairs constitute free, growing training set: lightweight surrogate trained on them can absorb significant portion of future traffic at near-zero marginal inference cost. The open questions are when the surrogate is reliable enough to deploy, what it handles versus defers, and how that boundary evolves as data accumulates. We introduce TRACER (Trace-based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing), an open-source system that trains ML surrogates on an LLMs own production traces and governs deployment through parity gate: the surrogate is activated only when its agreement with the LLM exceeds userspecified threshold α. To make the routing boundary transparent, TRACER generates interpretability artifacts describing which input regions the surrogate handles, where it plateaus, and why it defers. On 77-class intent benchmark with Sonnet 4.6 teacher, TRACER achieves 83100% surrogate coverage depending on the quality target α; on 150-class benchmark, the surrogate fully replaces the teacher. On natural language inference task, the parity gate correctly refuses deployment because the embedding representation cannot support reliable separation. The system is available as open-source software.1 Keywords: LLM routing, learning to defer, cost-efficient inference, surrogate models, parity gate, continual learning, explainability 1Independent Researcher. Correspondence to: Adam Rida <adamrida.ra@gmail.com>. Preprint. Work in progress. 1Code and documentation: github.com/adrida/tracer Production teams increasingly use LLMs as zero-shot classifiers to avoid the cost and delay of manual data labeling (Chae & Davidson, 2025; Sun et al., 2023). The appeal is clear: single prompt replaces months of annotation work. But every LLM classification call is billed, and at scale the per-query cost adds up quickly. Each call, however, also produces side effect: labeled inputoutput pair, already logged for billing and compliance, that can serve as training data. We call each such pair trace. In our experiments, surrogate trained on Sonnet 4.6 traces handles 100% of 150class intent traffic (157 effective classes including teacher label noise) with sub-millisecond CPU inference. The learning-to-defer (L2D) paradigm (Madras et al., 2018; Mozannar & Sontag, 2020) formalizes this idea: route easy inputs to cheap surrogate, defer hard ones to the teacher. But existing L2D and LLM-routing methods (Ding et al., 2024; Ong et al., 2025; Chen et al., 2023) require labeled data collected before deployment. TRACER removes this requirement: when the router defers to the teacher, the teachers response is the label. Although fewer deferrals mean less new training data, each remaining deferral provides signal from the surrogates uncertain regions, creating self-reinforcing teacher-trace flywheel. This flywheel raises two deployment questions. Safety: when is the surrogate reliable enough to go live? TRACER answers with parity gate that activates the surrogate only when its agreement with the teacher on held-out split exceeds threshold α. Trust: what does the surrogate handle versus defer? At each refit, interpretability artifacts describe the routing boundary, drawing on slice discovery (Chung et al., 2019), counterfactual contrasts (Wachter et al., 2017), and differential model explanations (Rida et al., 2023). Prior XAI methods explain individual predictions. TRACERs artifacts explain the routing partition: metalevel decision about which inputs the surrogate can reliably handle. Contributions. 1. An open-source, trace-driven routing system with paritygated deployment and continual learning flywheel that TRACER: Trace-Based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM Classification requires no manual labeling. acceptor : [0, 1], producing hybrid classifier h: 2. An alpha sweep across two intent benchmarks (77 and 157 classes) showing the coveragequality tradeoff, with negative result on NLI confirming the gates safety properties. 3. set of per-refit interpretability artifacts (slice summaries, boundary pairs) that describe the deferral partition rather than individual predictions. 2. Related Work Learning to defer. Madras et al. (2018) and Mozannar & Sontag (2020) frame deferral as joint classifierrejector optimization; Mozannar et al. (2023) add consistent surrogate losses; Narasimhan et al. (2022) study post-hoc estimators. All assume ground-truth labels collected upfront, which is often the main bottleneck in production settings where labeling budgets are limited and label schemas evolve over time. TRACER sidesteps this requirement entirely by acquiring supervision from teacher traces accumulated during production. LLM routing and cascades. The cascade framework (Dohan et al., 2022) formalizes sequential model invocation. FrugalGPT (Chen et al., 2023) cascades LLMs by cost; Hybrid LLM (Ding et al., 2024) routes by predicted difficulty; RouteLLM (Ong et al., 2025) uses preference data; AutoMix (Madaan et al., 2024) uses self-verification. These route between LLMs; TRACER routes from an LLM to classical ML, strictly cheaper target. XAI for routing boundaries. Slice Finder (Chung et al., 2019) discovers underperforming data slices; counterfactual explanations (Wachter et al., 2017) highlight minimal input changes that flip decision; DeltaXplainer (Rida et al., 2023) explains differences between models across time via interpretable decision rules. These methods target classifier predictions. TRACER adapts them to the deferral partition: explaining what the surrogate handles versus defers. Summary. TRACER combines ideas from L2D, LLM routing, and XAI into single system that (i) requires no upfront labeled data, (ii) routes to classical ML rather than cheaper LLM, and (iii) makes the routing boundary inspectable. To the best of our knowledge, no prior system integrates all three. 3. Method 3.1. Problem Setting production classifier : (LLM API) generates trace (x, (x)) per call. TRACER fits surrogate and 2 (cid:40) h(x) = (x) (x) if a(x) τ, otherwise, (1) where routes each input to either the surrogate (handled) or the teacher (deferred to the LLM). The objective is to maximize coverage, Cov = Pr[a(x) τ ], subject to teacher agreement (TA), TA = Pr[f (x) = (x) a(x) τ ] α. 3.2. Routing Pipeline Surrogates operate on precomputed text embeddings. At each refit, TRACER trains pool of candidate classifiers (logistic regression, SGD linear classifiers, MLPs, decision trees, random forests, and extra-trees ensembles) and selects the best by teacher-label macro-F1 on held-out validation split. For each candidate surrogate, binary acceptor estimates whether the surrogate will agree with the teacher on given input. The acceptor is logistic regression trained on four confidence features derived from the surrogates output probability vector: the top-1 class probability, the top-2 probability, the margin between them, and the normalized entropy. natural alternative is to threshold the surrogates predict proba output directly (i.e., defer when the maximum class probability is below some threshold). We use separate acceptor because it can combine multiple signals of uncertainty: high top-1 probability with small margin (two classes competing) is less reliable than the same top-1 probability with large margin, and the acceptor learns this distinction. In practice, the acceptor provides tighter TA guarantees than raw probability thresholding at the same coverage level (see Banking77 at α = 0.95 in Table 1, where TRACER achieves 0.959 TA vs. the baselines 0.951). The threshold τ is calibrated on held-out split by sweeping all unique acceptor scores and selecting the value that maximizes coverage while maintaining TA α. Two pipeline families compete at each refit: Global, where single surrogate handles all traffic without acceptor gating (eligible only when the surrogates overall TA already exceeds α); and L2D (learning-to-defer), where the surrogate is paired with the acceptor gate so that only high-confidence predictions are served by the surrogate. The system selects whichever family achieves higher coverage while meeting the TA constraint. (The codebase also implements residual two-stage cascade; it was never selected as optimal in our experiments and is omitted here for clarity.) 3.3. Parity Gate Before candidate pipeline is promoted to production, it must pass parity gate evaluated on held-out shadow split never used for training or threshold calibration. The TRACER: Trace-Based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM Classification candidate achieving the highest coverage while meeting TA α is promoted only if it also clears 5% coverage floor (preventing degenerate pipelines). Two properties make this design robust. First, it is conservative: if no candidate clears the gate, the system falls back to full teacher reliance. Second, it is temporal: pipeline that fails on day may pass on later day once the label buffer contains more traces. 3.4. Continual Learning (The Flywheel) On day 1, every input is sent to the teacher, and all resulting traces enter the label buffer. TRACER fits first candidate pipeline and applies the parity gate. On subsequent days, the active pipeline routes traffic: high-confidence inputs are handled by the surrogate, low-confidence inputs are deferred to the teacher. Each call to tracer.update() merges the new traces and refits from scratch, ensuring that threshold calibration remains valid on the full accumulated dataset. The flywheel requires no manual labeling: every deferred call produces free training example, naturally biased toward the decision boundary where the surrogate needs the most signal. 3.5. Interpretability Artifacts At each refit, TRACER generates five artifact types describing the deferral boundary: (A) Slice summaries. For each teacher-assigned class label (and optionally for length bins), the system reports what fraction of traffic is handled by the surrogate versus deferred to the teacher, along with the per-slice TA. An operator can immediately see which categories the surrogate owns and which remain teacher-dependent without inspecting individual predictions. Grounded in automated slice discovery (Chung et al., 2019). (B) Representative example cards. For each routing group (handled and deferred) within each class, the system selects the input closest to the embedding centroid of that group. These examples give the operator concrete mental model of what typical handled traffic and typical deferred traffic look like in practice. (C) Contrastive boundary pairs. Pairs of inputs that share the same teacher label but receive opposite routing decisions: one handled with high acceptor score, one deferred with low score. By comparing two inputs that differ only in routing outcome, the operator can identify what feature of the input (phrasing ambiguity, topic sensitivity, lexical overlap with other classes) causes the surrogate to defer. Grounded in counterfactual explanations (Wachter et al., 2017). (D) Temporal deltas. After refit, per-label changes in handled rate compared to the previous routing state (available from the second refit onward). The operator can audit whether each refit is expanding coverage (positive delta) or regressing (negative delta), and which specific categories are moving. Inspired by DeltaXplainers (Rida et al., 2023) approach to explaining how model behavior changes over time. (E) Disagreement cards. Cases where the surrogate and teacher disagree on held-out data, grouped by the surrogates predicted class. For tasks that pass the parity gate, these highlight residual failure modes. For tasks that never pass the gate, they explain why the surrogate fails (e.g., the surrogate defaults to one class, revealing representation gap). 4. Experiments 4.1. Setup We run tracer.fit() and tracer.update() from the shipped open-source package. All inputs are embedded with BGE-large-en-v1.5 (1024 dimensions), precomputed offline. Teacher. All tasks use Claude Sonnet 4.6 as the teacher LLM. Cached teacher predictions are used throughout (no live API calls during evaluation). Tasks. Banking77 (Casanueva et al., 2020): 77-class intent classification. 10,003 train traces, 3,080 test. Teacher accuracy: 78.7% (train), 81.0% (test). CLINC150 (Larson et al., 2019): 150 in-scope intent classes. 18,000 train traces, 4,500 test. Teacher accuracy: 94.4% (train), 93.1% (test). The teacher generates 7 spurious labels not in the ground-truth label set, producing 157 effective classes in the trace data; the surrogate learns to reproduce these. MNLI (Williams et al., 2018): 3-class natural language inference (entailment, neutral, contradiction). Used as negative control with ground-truth labels as stand-in teacher (a generous upper bound). We subsample 2,000, 5,000, and 10,000 training traces from the full 392Kexample dataset; 9,815 test. Alpha sweep. Rather than selecting single α per task, we sweep α {0.80, 0.85, 0.90, 0.95} on each benchmark, mapping the coveragequality tradeoff. Protocol. Training data is divided into 5 equal daily batches. Day 1: tracer.fit() on the first batch. 3 TRACER: Trace-Based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM Classification Table 1. Alpha sweep results on held-out test sets. Coverage (Cov) and teacher agreement (TA) for TRACER and the confidencethreshold baseline. GT Acc is end-to-end accuracy against ground truth (surrogate on handled traffic, teacher on deferred). Test TA below target α despite passing calibration gate (see 5). Baseline TRACER Task Banking77 CLINC150 α .80 .85 .90 .95 .80 .85 .90 .95 Cov TA Cov TA .894 100% .894 100% 96.1% .912 83.2% .959 .930 100% .930 100% .930 100% 100% .930 100% .899 100% .899 99.8% .900 87.8% . 100% .942 100% .942 100% .942 98.4% .951 MNLI .85.95 0% 0% GT .804 .804 .804 .816 .924 .924 .924 .924 Days 25: tracer.update() with each subsequent batch. Final evaluation on the held-out test set. Baseline. Confidence-threshold deferral: logistic regression trained on all traces at once (not incrementally), deferring inputs below probability threshold swept to maximize coverage at each α. This baseline represents the best simple deferral method can achieve with full hindsight; TRACERs flywheel must reach this level starting from zero data. 4.2. Main Results Table 1 reveals three distinct regimes. Regime 1: Full offload with quality headroom (CLINC150). The surrogate achieves 100% coverage at all four alpha levels, including α = 0.95. This means logistic regression on BGE embeddings completely replaces Sonnet 4.6 on 150-class intent classification. The parity gate selects the Global method (no acceptor needed) because the surrogates calibration-set agreement (95.2%) exceeds the threshold. However, the test-set TA is 93.0%, below the α = 0.95 target despite clearing it on the calibration split (95.2%). This caltest gap is an important caveat: the parity gates guarantee is only as strong as the representativeness of the calibration split. In practice, practitioners should monitor test-time TA and re-calibrate periodically. Ground-truth end-to-end accuracy is 92.4%, comparable to the teacheronly baseline (93.1%). The confidence-threshold baseline also achieves higher TA on CLINC150 (0.942 vs. 0.930), reflecting the fact that the simpler method calibrates better on this particular task. the system achieves 100% coverage (Global method). At α = 0.90, the L2D pipeline activates: coverage drops to 96.1% as the acceptor gate defers 3.9% of traffic to maintain 91.2% teacher agreement. At α = 0.95, coverage drops further to 83.2% with 95.9% TA. This is the alpha knob working as designed: the operator chooses the quality bar, and the system adjusts how much traffic it handles. The confidence-threshold baseline, which trains on all 10,003 traces at once, achieves comparable or slightly higher coverage (87.8% vs. 83.2% at α = 0.95; 99.8% vs. 96.1% at α = 0.90). This is expected: the baseline has the advantage of hindsight, seeing the complete dataset in single batch. TRACERs flywheel reaches similar performance starting from 2,001 traces on Day 1, with the parity gate ensuring quality at every step. The systems value is not that it beats hindsight baseline on static split, but that it (i) begins offloading traffic from the first day of production, (ii) gates each refit with formal quality check, and (iii) generates artifacts explaining the routing boundary. At α = 0.95, TRACER achieves tighter TA than the baseline (0.959 vs. 0.951), reflecting the acceptors ability to selectively defer uncertain inputs rather than relying solely on classifier confidence. Regime 3: Correct refusal (MNLI). On 3-class NLI, the parity gate correctly refuses deployment at all alpha levels and all data scales tested (2,000, 5,000, and 10,000 ground-truth-labeled traces; α {0.85, 0.90, 0.95}). In all nine configurations, coverage is exactly 0%: no candidate pipeline clears the gate. This is generous test (ground-truth labels rather than noisy teacher labels), yet the surrogate still cannot learn the entailment/neutral/contradiction boundary from BGE embeddings alone. NLI requires compositional reasoning over premisehypothesis pairs that frozen sentence embedding cannot capture. The gates refusal is the correct behavior: it prevents bad surrogate from going live. Extending TRACER to tasks like NLI, where frozen embeddings are insufficient, is natural direction for future work; fine-tuning the encoder on accumulated traces could unlock new task families while the parity gate continues to serve as the safety check. 4.3. Flywheel Dynamics Table 2 shows the flywheel at its most demanding setting (α = 0.95). With 2,001 traces on Day 1, the surrogate handles 73.4% of 77-class intent traffic while maintaining TA above 0.95. Coverage grows to 83.4% by Day 4 as the surrogate sees more of the input distribution. The small dip on Day 3 (74.5%) reflects the refit-from-scratch recalibration; coverage recovers once more data arrives. Regime 2: Coveragequality tradeoff (Banking77). Banking77 is the more revealing benchmark. At α 0.85, At lower alpha targets, the flywheel converges faster. At α = 0.80, Banking77 reaches 100% coverage on Day 1 TRACER: Trace-Based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM Classification 100 90 80 70 ) % ( r C T TRACER (Banking77) Baseline (Banking77) TRACER (CLINC150) 0.8 0.85 0.9 0.95 Target α Figure 1. Coverage vs. target α on held-out test sets. Banking77 shows clear tradeoff: raising α from 0.85 to 0.95 reduces coverage from 100% to 83%. CLINC150 achieves 100% at all targets. Table 2. Banking77 flywheel at α = 0.95 (the strictest setting). Coverage grows from 73% to 83% as traces accumulate, with TA at or above the target. Day Traces Cov (cal) TA (cal) 1 2 3 4 5 2,001 4,001 6,002 8,002 10,003 Test set 73.4% 78.2% 74.5% 83.4% 82.6% 83.2% .953 .951 .951 .950 .951 .959 from 2,001 traces alone. CLINC150 at α = 0.95 starts at 57.4% coverage on Day 1 (3,600 traces) and reaches 100% by Day 2 (7,200 traces), with calibration TA dropping from 0.973 to 0.951 as the system shifts from the conservative L2D pipeline to the full-coverage Global pipeline. Cost projection. At Sonnet 4.6 pricing ($3/M input, $15/M output tokens), Banking77 costs approximately $2.60 per 1,000 teacher calls (estimating 800 input tokens for the prompt including 77 intent names, 15 output tokens). system routing 10K queries/day spends $26/day ($9,500/year). With TRACER at 83.2% coverage (α = 0.95), this drops to $4.40/day, an 83% cost reduction saving $7,900/year. At α = 0.80 (100% coverage), the cost drops to zero after trace collection, 100% reduction. For CLINC150, the surrogate fully replaces the teacher at all alpha levels, eliminating ongoing LLM costs entirely. 4.4. Artifact Inspection We inspect the interpretability artifacts to check whether they produce sensible output across the three regimes. This is qualitative inspection; rigorous evaluation of artifact utility would require user study with practitioners, which we leave to future work. We include the inspection here to illustrate the artifact design and to show that the outputs are non-trivial. Table 3. Contrastive boundary pairs from Banking77 at α = 0.95. Each pair shares the same teacher label but opposite routing decisions. Full output in Appendix B. Handled (score) Deferred (score) Label returned an item but dont see it on my account? (0.95) Why hasnt my return account? cleared my (0.00) refund not showing up What is the procedure for activating this card? (0.96) Are there ATM fees? (0.94) How do get started when get my card? (0.00) activate my card Do any of your machines provide cash from my home country? (0.00) atm support CLINC150 (full offload). Slice summaries show 100% handled rate across all 157 classes with uniform TA. No boundary pairs are generated (none exist at 100% coverage), which is the correct artifact behavior: the output reflects the routing state, not fixed template. The Global method selection is itself an interpretable signal: the surrogate is good enough everywhere that gating adds no value. Banking77 at α = 0.95 (partial offload). At 83.2% coverage, slice summaries reveal per-class variHandled rates range from ation in handled rates. 78.2% (card payment not recognised) to 96.4% (transaction charged twice), showing that the surrogate is weaker on intents with overlapping surface forms. Length has modest effect: short and medium inputs are handled at 90.791.0%, while long inputs drop to 88.3%. Boundary pairs show semantically meaningful contrasts. Table 3 lists three representative pairs from the Banking77 qualitative report. In each case, the handled input is direct and unambiguous, while the deferred input uses vaguer phrasing that could plausibly belong to neighboring intent class. The acceptor scores reflect this: handled inputs score 0.940.96, while deferred inputs score 0.00. MNLI (correct refusal). No qualitative report is generated because the parity gate never fires at any of the nine tested configurations. The absence of artifacts is itself informative: it confirms there is no viable routing boundary. Unlike Banking77, where the surrogate learns intent-specific clusters, MNLIs 3-class structure (entailment, neutral, contradiction) cannot be separated from sentence-pair embeddings because the class depends on the logical relation between premise and hypothesis, not on the topic or phrasing of either sentence alone. The artifacts direct the practitioner toward richer representation. Table 4 summarizes the artifact signatures across regimes. 5 TRACER: Trace-Based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM Classification Table 4. Artifact signatures across routing regimes. Operator action Artifact signature Regime ofFull fload Partial Refused slices All 100%. No boundary pairs. Global selected. Uneven slices. Boundary show pairs ambiguity-based deferral. No artifacts. never fires. Gate Ship. Monitor for drift. Review deferred classes. Collect more traces. Task needs richer encoder or is unsuitable for surrogates. 5. Discussion When TRACER works. The results show that intent classification, even at 77 or 157 classes, is well-served by frozen embeddings plus classical ML. This is because intent tasks have relatively clean class boundaries in embedding space. Recent surveys confirm that LLM-based zero-shot classification is increasingly adopted in production as way to bypass manual annotation (Chae & Davidson, 2025; Sun et al., 2023). The practical implication is that many of these systems are paying per-call LLM prices for tasks that classical ML can handle, and TRACER provides principled, safe way to offload that traffic. Calibrationtest gap. On CLINC150, the parity gate passes at α = 0.95 based on calibration TA (0.952), but test TA is 0.930. This gap means the gates guarantee does not fully transfer to unseen data. The most likely cause is that the calibration and test splits have slightly different difficulty distributions. practical mitigation is to set α conservatively above the desired test-time TA, or to use conformal calibration (Angelopoulos & Bates, 2021) for distribution-free coverage guarantees. We leave conformal integration to future work. When it does not. MNLIs failure demonstrates clear boundary. Tasks requiring compositional reasoning (entailment vs. contradiction) produce embeddings where class boundaries are not linearly separable, regardless of data volume. The parity gate is essential here: without it, practitioner might deploy bad surrogate. natural extension is fine-tuning the encoder on accumulated traces, which could extend the viable task range while the parity gate continues to serve as the safety check. Surrogate imitates teacher, not ground truth. The surrogate replicates the teachers behavior, including its errors. On Banking77, Sonnet 4.6 achieves only 78.7% train / 81.0% test ground-truth accuracy across 77 fine-grained intents (e.g., distinguishing card not working from contactless not working). This reflects the genuine difficulty of zero-shot 77-way classification, not prompt deficiency. At α = 0.80 (100% coverage), endto-end GT accuracy is 80.4%, close to the teachers 81.0% on the test set. At α = 0.95 (83.2% coverage), GT accuracy rises to 81.6% because the hardest inputs are deferred back to the teacher, matching the teacher-only baseline. TRACER does not claim to improve on the teacher; it claims to match it at fraction of the cost. An interesting corollary is that the deferred inputs, which the surrogate cannot confidently classify, constitute natural hard-sample set. These are precisely the inputs where human annotation effort would be most valuable, suggesting that TRACERs deferral log could double as cost-efficient active labeling strategy: instead of labeling random samples, practitioners could focus annotation budgets on the deferred partition to build ground-truth dataset where it matters most. Limitations and future work. The current evaluation points to concrete directions for future development. Broader task coverage: both positive benchmarks are intent classification tasks with well-separated embedding clusters. Extending to tasks with noisier labels, longer inputs, multi-label structure, or compositional reasoning (as MNLI suggests) will likely require encoder fine-tuning on accumulated traces, which the parity gate can govern safely. Stronger baselines: the confidence-threshold comparator is the simplest possible deferral method. Planned comparisons include learned routing methods (e.g., RouteLLM (Ong et al., 2025)) and distillation approaches (Hsieh et al., 2023). Embedding diversity: all experiments use BGE-large-env1.5; the coveragequality tradeoff may shift with different encoders, and systematic encoder comparison is planned. Artifact evaluation: the XAI artifacts are inspected qualitatively here; user study with practitioners would provide stronger evidence of their utility in real deployment workflows. Incremental updates: the system currently refits from scratch at each update; incremental fitting would reduce compute cost for large trace buffers. Conformal guarantees: replacing the empirical calibration threshold with conformal prediction (Angelopoulos & Bates, 2021) would provide distribution-free coverage guarantees, addressing the calibrationtest gap observed on CLINC150. 6. Conclusion LLMs are best understood not as classifiers but as labeling engines: their most valuable output is the training signal each call produces for downstream surrogates. TRACER operationalizes this insight. On Banking77 (77 classes), the alpha knob controls the coveragequality tradeoff from 100% coverage at α = 0.80 down to 83.2% at α = 0.95. On CLINC150 (157 classes), the surrogate fully replaces the teacher at all quality targets. On MNLI, the parity gate corTRACER: Trace-Based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM Classification rectly refuses deployment, demonstrating safety by design. Mozannar, H. and Sontag, D. Consistent estimators for open-source at learning to defer to an expert. In ICML, 2020. system is The as github.com/adrida/tracer. available
